author: John Chrysostom
book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.

classification.cautionReason: No major mainstream heresy warning is attached by the preview classifier.

lineage: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.

ccel.title: NPNF1-10. St. Chrysostom: Homilies on the Gospel of Saint Matthew

source.title: NPNF1-10. St. Chrysostom: Homilies on the Gospel of Saint Matthew

title: Matthew XIII. 53.

source.title: NPNF1-10. St. Chrysostom: Homilies on the Gospel of Saint Matthew

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: “And it came to pass, that, when Jesus had finished these parables, He departed thence.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Wherefore said He, “these”?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Because He was to speak others besides.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And wherefore, again, doth He depart?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Desiring to sow the word everywhere.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: “And when He was come into His own country, He taught them in their synagogue.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And what doth he now call His country?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: As it seems to me, Nazareth.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: “For He did not many mighty works there,”it is said, but in Capernaum He did miracles: wherefore He said also, “And thou, Capernaum, which art exalted unto Heaven, shalt be brought down to hell; for if the mighty works, which have been done in thee, had been done in Sodom, it would have remained until this day.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But having come there, while He slackens somewhat in His miracles; so as not to inflame them unto more envy, nor to condemn them more grievously, by the aggravation of their unbelief: He yet puts forth a doctrine, having no less of wonder in it than the miracles.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For these utterly senseless men, when they ought to have marvelled, and to have been amazed at the power of His words, they on the contrary hold Him cheap, because of him who seemed to be His father; yet we know they had many examples of these things in the former times, and from fathers of no note had seen illustrious children.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For so David was the son of a certain mean husbandman, Jesse; and Amos, the child of a goatherd, and himself a goatherd;and Moses too, the lawgiver, had a father very inferior to himself.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: When they therefore, for this especially, ought to adore and be amazed, that being of such parents He spake such things, it being quite manifest, that so it was not of man’s care, but of God’s grace: yet they, what things they should admire Him for, for those they despise Him.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: He is moreover continually frequenting the synagogues, lest if He were always abiding in the wilderness, they should the more accuse Him as making a schism, and fighting against their polity.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Being amazed therefore, and in perplexity, they said, “Whence hath this man this wisdom, and these powers?”either calling the miracles powers, or even the wisdom itself.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: “Is not this the carpenter’s son?”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: The greater then the marvel, and the more abundant the ground of amaze.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: “Is not His mother called Mary, and His brethren James, and Joses,and Simon, and Judas? and His sisters, are they not all with us?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Whence hath this man these things?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And they were offended in Him.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Seest thou that Nazareth was where He was discoursing?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: “Are not his brethren,” it is said, “such a one, and such a one?”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And what of this?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Why, by this especially you ought to have been led on to faith.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But envy you see is a poor base thing, and often falls foul of itself.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For what things were strange and marvellous, and enough to have gained them over, these offended them.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: What then saith Christ unto them?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: “A prophet,” saith He, “is not without honor, save in his own country, and in his own house: and He did not,” it is said, “many mighty works, because of their unbelief.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But Luke saith, “And He did not there many miracles.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And yet it was to be expected He should have done them.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For if the feeling of wonder towards Him was gaining ground (for indeed even there He was marvelled at), wherefore did He not do them?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Because He looked not to the display of Himself, but to their profit.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Therefore when this succeeded not, He overlooked what concerned Himself, in order not to aggravate their punishment.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And yet see after how long a time He came to them, and after how great a display of miracles: but not even so did they endure it, but were inflamed again with envy.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Wherefore then did He yet do a few miracles?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: That they might not say, “Physician, heal thyself.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: That they might not say, “He is a foe and an enemy to us, and overlooks His own;” that they might not say, “If miracles had been wrought, we also should have believed.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Therefore He both wrought them, and stayed: the one, that He might fulfill His own part; the other, that He might not condemn them the more.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And consider thou the power of His words, herein at least, that possessed as they were by envy, they did yet admire.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And as with regard to His works, they do not find fault with what is done, but feign causes which have no existence, slaying, “In Beelzebub He casteth out the devils;” even so here too, they find no fault with the teaching, but take refuge in the meanness of His race.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But mark thou, I pray thee, the Master’s gentleness, how He reviles them not, but with great mildness saith, “A prophet is not without honor, save in his own country.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And neither here did He stop, but added, “And in his own house.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: To me it appears, that with covert reference to His very own brethren, He made this addition.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But in Luke He puts examples also of this, saying, that neither did Elias come unto His own, but to the stranger widow; neither by Eliseus was any other leper healed, but the stranger Naaman;and Israelites neither received benefit, nor conferred benefit, but the foreigners.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And these things He saith, signifying in every instance their evil disposition, and that in His case nothing new is taking place.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: “At that time Herod the tetrarch heard of the fame of Jesus.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For Herod the king, this man’s father, he that slew the children, was dead.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But not without a purpose doth the evangelist signify the time, but to make thee observe also the haughtiness of the tyrant, and his thoughtlessness, in that not at the beginning did he inform himself about Christ, but after a very long time.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For such are they that are in places of power, and are encompassed with much pomp, they learn these things late, because they do not make much account of them.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But mark thou, I pray thee, how great a thing virtue is, that he was afraid of him even when dead, and out of his fear he speaks wisely even concerning a resurrection.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: “For he said,” it is mentioned, “unto his servants, This is John, whom I slew, he is risen from the dead, and therefore the mighty powers do work in him.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Seest thou the intensity of his fear? for neither then did he dare to publish it abroad, but he still speaks but to his own servants.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But yet even this opinion savored of the soldier, and was absurd.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For many besides had risen from the dead, and no one had wrought anything of the kind.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And his words seem to me to be the language both of vanity, and of fear.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For such is the nature of unreasonable souls, they admit often a mixture of opposite passions.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But Luke affirms that the multitudes said, “This is Elias, or Jeremias, or one of the old prophets,”but he, as uttering forsooth something wiser than the rest, made this assertion.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But it is probable that before this, in answer to them that said He was John (for many had said this too), he had denied it, and said, “I slew him,” priding himself and glorying in it.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For this both Mark and Luke report that he said, “John I beheaded.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But when the rumor prevailed, then he too saith the same as the people.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Then the evangelist relates to us also the history.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And what might his reason be for not introducing it as a subject by itself?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Because all their labor entirely was to tell what related to Christ, and they made themselves no secondary work besides this, except it were again to contribute to the same end.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Therefore neither now would they have mentioned the history were it not on Christ’s account, and because Herod said, “John is risen again.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But Mark saith, that Herod exceedingly honored the man, and this, when reproved.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: So great a thing is virtue.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Then his narrative proceeds thus: “For Herod had laid hold on John, and bound him, and put him in prison, for Herodias’ sake, his brother Philip’s wife.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For John said unto him, It is not lawful for thee to have her.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And when he would have put him to death, he feared the people, because they counted him as a prophet.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And wherefore doth he not address his discourse at all to her, but to the man?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Because it depended more on him.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But see how inoffensive he makes his accusation, as relating a history rather than bringing a charge.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: “But when Herod’s birth-day was kept,”saith he, “the daughter of Herodias danced before them,and pleased Herod.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: O diabolical revel!

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: O satanic spectacle!

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: O lawless dancing! and more lawless reward for the dancing.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For a murder more impious than all murders was perpetrated, and he that was worthy to be crowned and publicly honored, was slain in the midst, and the trophy of the devils was set on the table.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And the means too of the victory were worthy of the deeds done.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: “The daughter of Herodias,” it is said, “danced in the midst, and pleased Herod.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Whereupon he sworewith an oath to give her whatsoever she would ask.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And she being before instructed ofher mother, said, Give me here John Baptist’s head in a charger.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Her reproach is twofold; first, that she danced, then that she pleased him, and so pleased him, as to obtain even murder for her reward.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Seest thou how savage he was? how senseless? how foolish? in putting himself under the obligation of an oath, while to her he gives full power over her request.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But when he saw the evil actually ensuing, “he was sorry,”it is said; and yet in the first instance he had put him in bonds.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Wherefore then is he sorry?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Such is the nature of virtue, even amongst the wicked admiration and praises are its due.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But alas for her madness!

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: When she too ought to admire, yea, to bow down to him, for trying to redress her wrong, she on the contrary even helps to arrange the plot, and lays a snare, and asks a diabolical favor.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But he was afraid “for the oath’s sake,” it is said, “and them that sat at meat with him.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And how didst thou not fear that which is more grievous?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Surely if thou wast afraid to have witnesses of thy perjury, much more oughtest thou to fear having so many witnesses of a murder so lawless.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But as I think many are ignorant of the grievance itself, whence the murder had its origin, I must declare this too, that ye may learn the wisdom of the lawgiver.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: What then was the ancient law, which Herod indeed trampled on, but John vindicated?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: The wife of him that died childless was to be given to his brother.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For since death was an incurable ill, and all was contrived for life’s sake; He makes a law that the living brother should marry her, and should call the child that is born by the name of the dead, so that his house should not utterly perish.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For if the dead were not so much as to leave children, which is the greatest mitigation of death, the sorrow would be without remedy.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Therefore you see, the lawgiver devised this refreshment for those who were by nature deprived of children, and commanded the issue to be reckoned as belonging to the other.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But when there was a child, this marriage was no longer permitted.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: “And wherefore?” one may say, “for if it was lawful for another, much more for the brother.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: By no means.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For He will have men’s consanguinity extended, and the sources multiplied of our interest in each other.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Why then, in the case also of death without offspring, did not another marry her?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Because it would not so be accounted the child of the departed; but now his brother begetting it, the fiction became probable.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And besides, any other man had no constraining call to build up the house of the dead, but this had incurred the claim by relationship.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Forasmuch then as Herod had married his brother’s wife, when she had a child, therefore John blames him, and blames him with moderation, showing together with his boldness, his consideration also.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But mark thou, I pray thee, how the whole theatre was devilish.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For first, it was made up of drunkenness and luxury, whence nothing healthful could come.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Secondly, the spectators in it were depraved, and he that gave the banquet the worst transgressor of all.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Thirdly, there was the irrational pleasure.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Fourthly, the damsel, because of whom the marriage was illegal, who ought even to have hid herself, as though her mother were dishonored by her, comes making a show, and throwing into the shade all harlots, virgin as she was.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And the time again contributes no little to the reproof of this enormity.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For when he ought to be thanking God, that on that day He had brought him to light, then he ventures upon those lawless acts.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: When one in chains ought to have been freed by him, then he adds slaughter to bonds.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Hearken, ye virgins, or rather ye wives also, as many as consent to such unseemliness at other person’s weddings, leaping, and bounding, and disgracing our common nature.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Hearken, ye men too, as many as follow after those banquets, full of expense and drunkenness, and fear ye the gulf of the evil one.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For indeed so mightily did he seize upon that wretched person just then, that he sware even to give the half of his kingdom: this being Mark’s statement, “He sware unto her, Whatsoever thou shalt ask of me, I will give it thee, unto the half of my kingdom.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Such was the value he set upon his royal power; so was he once for all made captive by his passion, as to give up his kingdom for a dance.vilifying, reviling, insulting.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But not so the saints; they on the contrary mourn for such as sin, rather than curse them.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: This then let us also do, and let us weep for Herodias, and for them that imitate her.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For many such revels now also take place, and though John be not slain, yet the members of Christ are, and in a far more grievous way.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For it is not a head in a charger that the dancers of our time ask, but the souls of them that sit at the feast.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For in making them slaves, and leading them to unlawful loves, and besetting them with harlots, they do not take off the head, but slay the soul, making them adulterers, and effeminate, and whoremongers.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For thou wilt not surely tell me, that when full of wine, and drunken, and looking at a woman who is dancing and uttering base words, thou dost not feel anything towards her, neither art hurried on to profligacy, overcome by thy lust.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Nay, that awful thing befalls thee, that thou “makest the members of Christ members of an harlot.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For though the daughter of Herodias be not present, yet the devil, who then danced in her person, in theirs also holds his choirs now, and departs with the souls of those guests taken captive.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But if ye are able to keep clear of drunkenness, yet are ye partakers of another most grievous sin; such revels being also full of much rapine.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For look not, I pray thee, on the meats that are set before them, nor on the cakes; but consider whence they are gathered, and thou wilt see that it is of vexation, and covetousness, and violence, and rapine.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: “Nay, ours are not from such sources,” one may say.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: God forbid they should be: for neither do I desire it.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Nevertheless, although they be clear of these, not even so are our costly feasts freed from blame.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Hear, at all events, how even apart from these things the prophet finds fault with them, thus speaking, “Woe to them that drink wine racked off, and anoint themselves with the chief ointments.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Seest thou how He censures luxury too?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For it is not covetousness which He here lays to their charge, but prodigality only.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And thou eatest to excess, Christ not even for need; thou various cakes, He not so much as dry bread; thou drinkest Thasian wine, but on Him thou hast not bestowed so much as a cup of cold water in His thirst.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Thou art on a soft and embroidered bed, but He is perishing with the cold.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Wherefore, though the banquets be clear from covetousness, yet even so are they accursed, because, while for thy part thou doest all in excess, to Him thou givest not even His need; and that, living in luxury upon things that belong to Him.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Why, if thou wert guardian to a child, and having taken possession of his goods, were to neglect him in extremities, thou wouldest have ten thousand accusers, and wouldest suffer the punishment appointed by the laws; and now having taken possession of the goods of Christ, and thus consuming them for no purpose, dost thou not think thou wilt have to give account?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And these things I say not of those who introduce harlots to their tables (for to them I have nothing to say, even as neither have I to the dogs), nor of those who cheat some, and pamper others (for neither with them have I anything to do, even as I have not with the swine and with the wolves); but of those who enjoy indeed their own property, but do not impart thereof to others; of those who spend their patrimony at random.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For neither are these clear from reprehension.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For how, tell me, wilt thou escape reprov ing and blame, while thy parasite is pampered, and the dog that stands by thee, but Christ’s worth appears to thee even not equal to theirs? when the one receives so much for laughter’s sake, but the other for the Kingdom of Heaven not so much as the smallest fraction thereof.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And while the parasite, on saying something witty, goes away filled; this Man, who hath taught us, what if we had not learnt we should have been no better than the dogs,—is He counted unworthy of even the same treatment with such an one?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Dost thou shudder at being told it?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Shudder then at the realities.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Cast out the parasites, and make Christ to sit down to meat with thee.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: If He partake of thy salt, and of thy table, He will be mild in judging thee: He knows how to respect a man’s table.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Yea, if robbers know this, much more the Lord.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Think, for instance, of that harlot, how at a table He justified her, and upbraids Simon, saying, “Thou gavest me no kiss.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: I say, if He feed thee, not doing these things, much more will He reward thee, doing them.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Look not at the poor man, that he comes to thee filthy and squalid, but consider that Christ by him is setting foot in thine house, and cease from thy fierceness, and thy relentless words, with which thou art even aspersing such as come to thee, calling them impostors, idle, and other names more grievous than these.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And think, when thou art talking so, of the parasites; what kind of works do they accomplish? in what respect do they profit thine house?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Do they really make thy dinner pleasant to thee? pleasant, by their being beaten and saying foul words?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Nay, what can be more unpleasing than this, when thou smitest him that is made after God’s likeness, and from thine insolence to him gatherest enjoyment for thyself, making thine house a theatre, and filling thy banquet with stage-players, thou who art well born and free imitating the actors with their heads shaven?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For among them too is laughter, and rude blows.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: These things then dost thou call pleasure, I pray thee, which are deserving of many tears, of much mourning and lamentation?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And when it were fit to urge them to a good life, to give timely advice, dost thou lead them on to perjuries, and disorderly language, and call the thing a delight? and that which procures hell, dost thou account a subject of pleasure?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Yea, and when they are at a loss for witty sayings, they pay the whole reckoning with oaths and false swearing.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Are these things then worthy of laughter, and not of lamentations and tears?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Nay, who would say so, that hath understanding?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And this I say, not forbidding them to be fed, but not for such a purpose.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Nay, let their maintenance have the motive of kindness, not of cruelty; let it be compassion, not insolence.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Because he is a poor man, feed him; because Christ is fed, feed him; not for introducing satanical sayings, and disgracing his own life.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Look not at him outwardly laughing, but examine his conscience, and then thou wilt see him uttering ten thousand imprecations against himself, and groaning, and wailing.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And if he do not show it, this also is due to thee.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Let the companions of thy meals then be men that are poor and free, not perjured persons, nor stage-players.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And if thou must needs ask of them a requital for their food, enjoin them, should they see anything done that is amiss, to rebuke, to admonish, to help thee in thy care over thine household, in the government of thy servants.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Hast thou children?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Let these be joint fathers to them, let them divide thy charge with thee, let them yield thee such profits as God loveth.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Engage them in a spiritual traffic.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And if thou see one needing protection, bid them succor, command them to minister.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: By these do thou track the strangers out, by these clothe the naked, by these send to the prison, put an end to the distresses of others.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Let them give thee, for their food, this requital, which profits both thee and them, and carries with it no condemnation.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Hereby friendship also is more closely riveted.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For now, though they seem to be loved, yet for all that they are ashamed, as living without object in thy house; but if they accomplish these purposes, both they will be more pleasantly situated, and thou wilt have more satisfaction in maintaining them, as not spending thy money without fruit; and they again will dwell with thee in boldness and due freedom, and thy house, instead of a theatre, will become to thee a church, and the devil will be put to flight, and Christ will enter, and the choir of the angels.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For where Christ is, there are the angels too, and where Christ and the angels are, there is Heaven, there is a light more cheerful than this of the sun.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And if thou wouldest reap yet another consolation through their means, command them, when thou art at leisure, to take their books and read the divine law.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: They will have more pleasure in so ministering to you, than in the other way.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For these things add respect both to thee and to them, but those bring disgrace upon all together; upon thee as an insolent person and a drunkard, upon them as wretched and gluttonous.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For if thou feed in order to insult them, it is worse than if thou hadst put them to death; but if for their good and profit, it is more useful again than if thou hadst brought them back from their way to execution.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And now indeed thou dost disgrace them more than thy servants, and thy servants enjoy more liberty of speech, and freedom of conscience, than they do; but then thou wilt make them equal to the angels.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Set free therefore both them and thine own self, and take away the name of parasite, and call them companions of thy meals;cast away the appellation of flatterers, and bestow on them that of friends.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: With this intent indeed did God make our friendships, not for evil to the beloved and loving, but for their good and profit.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But these friendships are more grievous than any enmity.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For by our enemies, if we will, we are even profited; but by these we must needs be harmed, no question of it.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Keep not then friends to teach thee harm; keep not friends who are enamored rather of thy table than of thy friendship.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For all such persons, if thou retrench thy good living, retrench their friendship too; but they that associate with thee for virtue’s sake, remain continually, enduring every change.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And besides, the race of the parasites doth often take revenge upon thee, and bring upon thee an ill fame.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Hence at least I know many respectable persons to have got bad characters, and some have been evil reported of for sorceries, some for adulteries and corrupting of youths.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For whereas they have no work to do, but spend their own life unprofitably; their ministry is suspected by the multitude as being the same with that of corrupt youths.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Therefore, delivering ourselves both from evil report, and above all from the hell that is to come, and doing the things that are well-pleasing to God, let us put an end to this devilish custom, that “both eating and drinking we may do all things to the glory of God,”and enjoy the glory that cometh from Him; unto which may we all attain, by the grace and love towards man of our Lord Jesus Christ, to whom be glory and might, now and ever, and world without end.
